logs.title: Logs
logs.subtitle: Collect, review, and analyze application logs in one place

guide.title: How to Collect Logs
guide.subtitle: Start collecting logs with your existing logger configuration or log files. No separate SDK is required.

step1.title: Create a Log Service
step1.desc: Click "Add Log" and create a service name. A dedicated API key is generated automatically for that service.

step2.title: Choose an Integration Method
step2.desc: Open the Integration tab and choose the method that fits your environment. Use HTTP Appender when you can edit app code, or Log Agent when you want to collect log files or stdout from servers and containers.

step3.title: Review Logs and Add Alerts
step3.desc: Collected logs appear in the log list right away. If needed, add Telegram or Discord alert rules for error and warn logs.

logs.pausedMessage: Log updates are paused. Click Resume to restart.

detail.truncated: Truncated — showing first {{shown}} bytes of {{total}} bytes total

empty.title: No requests captured
empty.description: No API requests captured yet. Configure capture settings to get started.

settings.title: API Request Capture
settings.subtitle: Configure how requests and responses are captured for this service.

mode.label: Capture Mode
mode.sampledDesc: Capture a sample of all requests.

sampleRate.label: Sample Rate (%)
sampleRate.description: Percentage of successful responses to capture (0–100)

maskedBodyFields.label: Masked Body Fields
maskedBodyFields.description: JSON body field names to mask

logServices.emptyDesc: Add a log service, then start collecting logs with HTTP Appender or Log Agent.

add.title: Add Log
add.infoDesc: Create a log service to generate an API key automatically. Then choose HTTP Appender or Log Agent in the Integration tab and connect your app.

validation.idFormat: Only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens

toast.createdGuide: Log added! Copy the integration code below to start collecting logs.

loggingLibrary.title: Send Directly From App Code
loggingLibrary.desc: Add HTTP sending to your existing logging library such as Winston, Logback, or Serilog. This is usually the easiest option when you can modify application code.

http.title: Direct HTTP POST
http.desc: Send logs with plain HTTP POST from any language or platform. This works well for scripts or custom log forwarding logic.

identity.levelFilterHint: Click to go to the Settings tab

delete.title: Delete Log
delete.subtitle: This action cannot be undone
delete.confirm: Are you sure you want to delete

apiKey.revealDesc: Copy your new API key now. It will not be shown again.
apiKey.revealOnce: This key is shown only once. Copy it now - you won't be able to see it again.

settings.levelFilterDesc: Choose which log levels this service stores. Unselected levels are silently dropped at ingest time and do not count as agent errors.
settings.selectAtLeastOne: Select at least one log level.
settings.summaryNone: No levels selected - nothing will be collected

picker.title: What do you want to collect?
picker.subtitle: Choose the method that matches the data you want to capture.
picker.notSureTitle: Not sure which one to choose?
picker.notSureDesc: Use Log Agent to collect log files or stdout. Use API Request Capture to record HTTP request and response data.

agent.label: Log Agent
agent.tagline: A Docker container that tails and forwards your log files
agent.description: Mount your log directory and run the container. No changes to your application code.

apiCapture.label: API Request Capture
apiCapture.tagline: Add middleware to capture HTTP requests and responses
apiCapture.description: Add a middleware to Spring Boot, FastAPI, Express, and more. Captures method, path, status, duration, and body with no infrastructure changes.
goodFor: Spring Boot / FastAPI / Express / Go / Django

httpAppender.label: HTTP Appender
httpAppender.tagline: Add an HTTP endpoint to your app logger
httpAppender.description: Add an HTTP transport to Winston, Logback, Serilog, or Python logging. Control what gets sent from your code.

endpoint.title: Ingest endpoint
endpoint.description: Accepts common logger formats and batches up to 100 logs per request.
endpoint.apiCaptureDescription: Accepts up to 50 API request entries per batch. Use the X-API-Key header for authentication.

test.title: Test the connection first
test.description: Send one small log entry before changing production logging. The status updates when a log arrives.
test.troubleshootingDesc: If the request reaches a reverse proxy, make sure the Authorization header is forwarded.

setup.agentDesc: Mount the log directory and start the EveryUp Log Agent.
setup.httpDesc: Pick your framework and add the HTTP transport to your logger.
setup.apiCaptureDesc: Pick your framework and add the middleware to your application.
setup.agentHint: Quick start is enough for most setups. Change the mounted path to the directory where your application writes logs.
setup.levelPrefixHint: For plain text logs, prefix lines with [INFO], [WARN], or [ERROR] so EveryUp can classify levels correctly. JSON logs can use level, severity, logLevel, or lvl fields.
setup.apiCaptureHint: Go to the Settings tab → API Capture and enable a capture mode before data will be collected.
setup.otherOptionsDesc: Fluent Bit config, Docker sidecar, Docker pipe, and systemd examples.

modals.regenerateDesc: The previous key stops working immediately. Update any running agents or appenders.
modals.newKeyDesc: Copy it now. It will be hidden again shortly.

toast.regenerateFirst: Regenerate the API key first, then try the browser test.
